1:
"And the apostles and brethren that were in Judaea heard that the Gentiles had also received the word of God." The witnesses note the news of the Gentiles' conversion reaching the Judean churches. Gill notes "the apostles and brethren that were in Judea" — the rest of the twelve and the members of the churches in Judea, "for there were in it now more churches than that at Jerusalem"; the news of the great event spreading among them. The witnesses note that the report of Cornelius and his household receiving the gospel — the Gentiles admitted to the faith — reached the believers in Judea, stirring deep interest and (as the next verses show) some objection. The witnesses note the news of the Gentiles' conversion. The witnesses note the report reaching Judea.

The witnesses note the news of the Gentiles receiving the word reaching the Judean churches — a momentous report.

So the witnesses make the verse the news of the Gentiles' conversion reaching Judea. The lesson the expositors draw is the momentous nature of the event and the stir it caused among the Jewish believers. The witnesses note the spread of the report: "the apostles and brethren that were in Judaea heard that the Gentiles had also received the word of God" — Gill notes the apostles (the rest of the twelve) and the members of the churches in Judea (now more than the one at Jerusalem) heard of the great event; the news of Cornelius and his household receiving the gospel spreading among the believers. The expositors observe the momentousness of the report: the Gentiles — the uncircumcised, long excluded — had received the word of God, believed, and been baptized; a turning-point in the history of the church, the gospel breaking through to the nations; the news naturally exciting deep interest and discussion. The witnesses press the significance of "the Gentiles had also received the word of God": not the Jews only, nor the Samaritans (a kindred people), but the Gentiles — wholly outside the covenant — had now received the gospel; the middle wall beginning to fall (Ephesians 2:14); the great enlargement of the church to all nations. The expositors note the stir it caused: the report, momentous and unexpected, would provoke not only interest but objection (the next verse); the deep-rooted Jewish prejudice against the Gentiles making the news a matter of controversy; the church to be tested whether it would receive the Gentiles. The witnesses observe the providence in the spread of the news: the report reached Judea, that the question of the Gentiles' admission might be raised, examined, and settled; the controversy the occasion for the church to embrace, by the manifest will of God, the inclusion of the Gentiles. The expositors note the importance of the event for the whole future of the church: the admission of the Gentiles, here first reported, would shape the gospel's advance to all nations; the news of Cornelius the first public token of the church's catholicity. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the momentous news of the Gentiles' conversion, the stir it caused, and the providence that brought the question before the church. The verse thus records the news of the Gentiles receiving the word reaching Judea — a momentous report — teaching the great enlargement of the church to the Gentiles and the controversy it would occasion.
2:
"And when Peter was come up to Jerusalem, they that were of the circumcision contended with him," The witnesses note the objection of the circumcision party against Peter. Barnes draws a pointed inference: "this is one of the circumstances which show conclusively that the apostles and early Christians did not regard Peter as having any particular supremacy" — for "they that were of the circumcision contended with him, disputed, or reproved him, charged him with being in fault." Gill notes the journey from Caesarea to Jerusalem. The witnesses note the Jewish believers (the circumcision party) contending with Peter over his going to the Gentiles — a striking proof that Peter was not regarded as supreme. The witnesses note the contention with Peter. The witnesses note the circumcision party.

The witnesses note the circumcision party contending with Peter — a proof he was not regarded as supreme.

So the witnesses make the verse the objection of the circumcision party. The lesson the expositors draw is the freedom with which the early church called even Peter to account, and the Jewish prejudice that occasioned the dispute. The witnesses note the objectors: "they that were of the circumcision contended with him" — the Jewish believers, those zealous for the circumcision and the law, contended with Peter, disputed with him, reproved him, charged him with being in fault for going to the Gentiles. The expositors press Barnes's pointed inference: this is a circumstance showing conclusively that the apostles and early Christians did not regard Peter as having any particular supremacy or primacy; for the believers freely contended with him, disputed and reproved him, called him to account — which they would not have done had they held him the head of the church, infallible or supreme; the free reproof of Peter a witness against later claims of his primacy. The witnesses observe the nature of the objection: the circumcision party objected not to the Gentiles' conversion as such, but to Peter's going in to the uncircumcised and eating with them (the next verse); their prejudice against fellowship with Gentiles, rooted in the law and custom of separation, the ground of their complaint. The expositors draw the lesson on the deep-rooted Jewish prejudice: the Jewish believers, still holding the old separation, were scandalized that Peter had associated with Gentiles; the prejudice so strong that even the conversion of the Gentiles was overshadowed, in their minds, by the breach of the separation; the difficulty of overcoming inbred prejudice, even in believers. The witnesses note the freedom and order of the early church: the believers freely raised their objection, and Peter answered it with a plain account (the next verses); the matter examined openly, not by the authority of one, but by the evidence of God's will; the church settling the question by argument and fact, not by decree. The expositors observe the providence in the controversy: the objection drew out Peter's full account of God's dealings (vv.4-17), establishing the inclusion of the Gentiles on the clear evidence of God's will; the dispute the occasion of the truth's confirmation. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the free reproof of Peter (against his supposed supremacy), the deep Jewish prejudice, and the church's settling of the question by evidence. The verse thus records the circumcision party contending with Peter — a proof he was not supreme — teaching the freedom of the early church and the prejudice that occasioned the dispute.
3:
"Saying, Thou wentest in to men uncircumcised, and didst eat with them." The witnesses note the specific charge against Peter. Clarke explains the offense: "in a Jew, this was no small offense; and, as they did not know the reason of St. Peter's conduct, it is no wonder they should call him to account for it, as they considered it to be a positive transgression of the law and custom of the Jews." Gill notes Peter "went into the houses of such, and lodged with them, and familiarly conversed with them." The witnesses note the charge — that Peter went in to the uncircumcised and ate with them — the breach of the Jewish separation that scandalized the circumcision party. The witnesses note the charge of eating with Gentiles. The witnesses note the breach of separation.

The witnesses note the charge: Peter went in to the uncircumcised and ate with them — the breach of Jewish separation.

So the witnesses make the verse the specific charge against Peter. The lesson the expositors draw is the depth of the Jewish prejudice against fellowship with Gentiles, which made Peter's association with them a grave offense in their eyes. The witnesses note the charge: "Thou wentest in to men uncircumcised, and didst eat with them" — Peter was charged with going into the houses of the uncircumcised Gentiles, lodging with them, conversing familiarly, and eating with them; the breach of the strict Jewish separation from the Gentiles. The expositors observe the gravity of this in Jewish eyes, which Clarke marks: in a Jew, this was no small offense; to eat with the uncircumcised was held a positive transgression of the law and custom of the Jews, who kept themselves separate from the Gentiles, regarding their food and fellowship as defiling; and the circumcision party, not knowing the reason of Peter's conduct (the vision and the Spirit's leading), naturally called him to account. The witnesses press the depth of the prejudice: the separation from the Gentiles was so deeply ingrained that to eat with them seemed a defilement, a breach of the law; the Jewish believers, still bound by this prejudice, were scandalized that Peter had so associated with Gentiles. The expositors draw the lesson on the barrier the gospel had to break: the separation between Jew and Gentile, rooted in centuries of law and custom, was a deep and stubborn barrier; the gospel, to embrace the Gentiles, had to overcome this prejudice even in the believers; the difficulty of the church's enlargement. The witnesses observe the irony the circumcision party did not see: that the very thing they charged as a transgression — Peter's eating with the Gentiles — was the sign and fruit of the gospel's reaching the nations; the breach of the old separation the opening of the new fellowship; what they saw as defilement God had cleansed (v.9). The expositors note the reasonableness of their objection given their ignorance: not knowing the vision and the Spirit's command, the circumcision party reasonably objected; their charge to be answered by Peter's account of God's dealings (the next verses); the prejudice to be overcome by the evidence of God's will. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the depth of the Jewish prejudice, the barrier the gospel had to break, and the breach of the old separation as the opening of the new fellowship. The verse thus records the charge against Peter — going in to the uncircumcised and eating with them — teaching the depth of the Jewish prejudice and the barrier the gospel had to break to reach the Gentiles.
4:
"But Peter rehearsed the matter from the beginning, and expounded it by order unto them, saying," The witnesses note Peter's calm, orderly answer. Barnes draws the lesson from Peter's method: "a simple and unvarnished statement of facts is usually the best way of disarming prejudice and silencing opposition. In revivals of true religion, the best way of silencing opposition... is to make a plain statement of things as they actually occurred"; opposition arises from prejudice or false report, and is best removed "not by angry contention, but by an unvarnished relation of the facts." The witnesses note Peter answering the charge not with anger, but by rehearsing the whole matter in order — the plain account of God's dealings. The witnesses note Peter's orderly answer. The witnesses note the plain statement of facts.

The witnesses note Peter answering with a calm, orderly account — disarming prejudice by a plain statement of facts.

So the witnesses make the verse Peter's calm, orderly answer. The lesson the expositors draw is the wisdom of answering prejudice not with anger but with a plain, orderly statement of facts. The witnesses note Peter's method: "Peter rehearsed the matter from the beginning, and expounded it by order unto them" — Peter, instead of resenting the charge or contending angrily, calmly rehearsed the whole matter from the beginning, in order, giving a plain account of all that had happened — the vision, the Spirit's command, Cornelius's angel, the Spirit falling on the Gentiles. The expositors press Barnes's lesson on the wisdom of this: a simple, unvarnished statement of facts is usually the best way to disarm prejudice and silence opposition; for opposition commonly arises from prejudice or from false and exaggerated reports, and is best removed not by angry contention but by a plain relation of what actually occurred; the facts, well told, dispel the prejudice. The witnesses observe the meekness of Peter: an apostle, charged and reproved by the believers, does not stand on his authority or resent the reproof, but humbly and patiently explains himself; the meekness that answers objection with reason, not with rank; a pattern of humility in one falsely thought supreme. The expositors draw the lesson on the right way to meet opposition: not with anger, contention, or assertion of authority, but with a calm, orderly, truthful account; the plain statement of God's dealings the best answer to prejudice; reason and fact, not heat and pride, the way to peace. The witnesses note the orderliness of Peter's account: he expounded it "by order" — beginning at the beginning, relating the events in sequence; the clear, orderly narration that lets the facts speak; the truth, plainly and fully told, carrying its own conviction. The expositors observe the effect this would have (v.18): the plain account silenced the objectors and brought them to glorify God; the facts, well stated, disarming the prejudice and winning assent; the wisdom of Peter's method vindicated by its success. The witnesses draw the application: the believer learns from Peter to meet objection with a calm, orderly, truthful account; to disarm prejudice by the plain statement of facts; and to answer with meekness, not anger or assertion of rank. The verse thus records Peter's orderly answer — rehearsing the matter from the beginning — teaching the wisdom of disarming prejudice by a plain, orderly statement of facts.
5:
"I was in the city of Joppa praying: and in a trance I saw a vision, A certain vessel descend, as it had been a great sheet, let down from heaven by four corners; and it came even to me:" The witnesses note Peter's recounting of the vision of the sheet. Ellicott and Robertson note the touches of vivid personal recollection in Peter's retelling — the sheet coming "even to me" (a detail not in the first account, Acts 10:11), the marks of an eyewitness recalling the scene. Wesley notes the trance "suspends the use of the outward senses." The witnesses note Peter beginning his account with the vision at Joppa — the great sheet let down from heaven, the divine preparation for the Gentile mission. The witnesses note the vision of the sheet. The witnesses note the vivid recollection.

The witnesses note Peter recounting the vision of the sheet — the divine preparation for the Gentile mission.

So the witnesses make the verse Peter's recounting of the vision of the sheet. The lesson the expositors draw is the divine origin of the Gentile mission, shown in the vision that God gave Peter. The witnesses note the setting: "I was in the city of Joppa praying" — Peter begins his account with the vision granted him while at prayer; the revelation coming in the season of devotion, God meeting the praying apostle with the vision that would open the Gentile mission. The expositors observe the vision: "in a trance I saw a vision, A certain vessel descend, as it had been a great sheet, let down from heaven by four corners" — the great sheet let down from heaven, the vessel that would teach Peter that God had cleansed what the law called unclean; the divine preparation for his going to the Gentiles. The witnesses note the touches of vivid personal recollection that the moderns mark: Ellicott and Robertson observe that Peter's retelling adds touches of an eyewitness recalling the scene — the sheet coming "even to me" (a detail not in the first account, Acts 10:11), the vividness of personal memory; the apostle relating what he himself had seen and felt. The expositors draw the lesson on the divine origin of the mission: Peter begins not with his own reasoning or choice, but with the vision God gave — establishing that the Gentile mission was not his invention but God's revelation; the whole proceeding grounded in the manifest will of God, shown in the vision. The witnesses observe Wesley's note on the trance: the trance suspended the use of the outward senses, so that Peter, in this state, received the vision from God; the revelation given in a manner that marked it as divine, not the working of his own mind. The expositors note the wisdom of Peter's beginning here: by starting with the vision, Peter shows the objectors that his going to the Gentiles was commanded by God, not chosen by himself; the vision the first and decisive evidence of God's will; the answer to the charge grounded in God's own revelation. The witnesses draw the lesson on the divine guidance of the church's mission: the great step of the Gentile mission was directed by God, through the vision; the church's enlargement the work of God, not the device of men; the divine origin the warrant for the mission. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the divine origin of the Gentile mission, the vision God gave the praying apostle, and the vivid recollection of the eyewitness. The verse thus records Peter's recounting of the vision of the sheet — the great vessel let down from heaven — teaching the divine origin of the Gentile mission and the revelation that prepared it.
6:
"Upon the which when I had fastened mine eyes, I considered, and saw fourfooted beasts of the earth, and wild beasts, and creeping things, and fowls of the air." The witnesses note Peter's earnest gaze and what the sheet contained. Ellicott and Robertson again mark the vividness: "the Apostle recalls the intense eager gaze with which he had looked on the strange vision." Gill notes the eyes were "not of his body, but of his mind; for this was only in vision." The witnesses note the sheet full of all kinds of creatures, clean and unclean together — the vision that would teach Peter that the distinction of clean and unclean was abolished, and the Gentiles not to be called common. The witnesses note the creatures in the sheet. The witnesses note the earnest gaze.

The witnesses note the sheet full of all creatures, clean and unclean — the vision abolishing the old distinction.

So the witnesses make the verse the contents of the sheet. The lesson the expositors draw is the meaning of the mingled creatures — the abolition of the distinction of clean and unclean, prefiguring the admission of the Gentiles. The witnesses note Peter's earnest attention: "when I had fastened mine eyes, I considered" — Ellicott and Robertson mark the vividness, the intense, eager gaze with which Peter had looked on the strange vision; Gill notes the eyes were of the mind, the vision being a mental, not a bodily, sight; the apostle's earnest consideration of what he saw. The expositors observe the contents: "fourfooted beasts of the earth, and wild beasts, and creeping things, and fowls of the air" — the sheet contained all kinds of creatures, clean and unclean together, the animals the law forbade as well as those it allowed; the mingling of clean and unclean in the one sheet. The witnesses press the meaning of the mingled creatures: the vision, with its clean and unclean animals together, was to teach Peter that the old distinction of clean and unclean (Leviticus 11) was abolished; and, more deeply, that the Gentiles, whom the Jews counted unclean and common, were not to be so regarded; the animals a figure of the peoples, the abolition of the food-distinction a figure of the abolition of the separation between Jew and Gentile. The expositors draw the lesson on the abolition of the ceremonial distinction: the ceremonial law, with its clean and unclean, was fulfilled and set aside in Christ; the vision declaring the distinction abolished, the wall of separation broken; "there is no difference between the Jew and the Greek" (Romans 10:12). The witnesses observe the deeper meaning for the Gentiles: the unclean animals, now to be eaten (v.7), figured the Gentiles, now to be received; the vision preparing Peter to count no man common or unclean (Acts 10:28); the abolition of the food-law the sign of the abolition of the separation. The expositors note the providence in the vision: God taught Peter, by this striking figure, the great truth he must act on — that the Gentiles were to be received; the vision the divine instruction, the figure preparing the apostle for the Gentile mission. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the mingled creatures, the abolition of the old distinction, and the vision that prepared Peter to receive the Gentiles. The verse thus records the contents of the sheet — all creatures, clean and unclean — teaching the abolition of the old distinction and the admission of the Gentiles it prefigured.
7:
"And I heard a voice saying unto me, Arise, Peter; slay and eat." The witnesses note the voice commanding Peter to eat. Gill notes Peter "apprehended in his mind" the voice, hearing it as Paul heard when in a trance. The grammatical-historical Robertson discusses the construction of "a voice saying." The witnesses note the heavenly voice commanding Peter to arise, kill, and eat — the command to disregard the old distinction of clean and unclean, the test of his obedience and the lesson of the abolished separation. The witnesses note the command to eat. The witnesses note the heavenly voice.

The witnesses note the voice commanding Peter to slay and eat — the command to disregard the old distinction.

So the witnesses make the verse the voice commanding Peter to eat. The lesson the expositors draw is the divine command to disregard the old distinction of clean and unclean, testing Peter's obedience and teaching the abolished separation. The witnesses note the command: "I heard a voice saying unto me, Arise, Peter; slay and eat" — a heavenly voice, which Peter apprehended in his mind (Gill notes, as Paul heard in his trance), commanded him to arise, kill, and eat of the creatures in the sheet — including the unclean animals the law forbade. The expositors observe the meaning of the command: Peter was commanded to do what the law had forbidden — to eat of the unclean; the command declaring the old distinction abolished, the food-law set aside; a direct, divine command to disregard the ceremonial separation. The witnesses press the test this posed: the command tested Peter's obedience — would he, a strict observer of the law (v.8), obey the heavenly voice against his lifelong scruples? the command crossing his deepest prejudice, testing whether he would submit to God's new revelation. The expositors draw the lesson on the abolition of the food-law: the command to "slay and eat" of all, clean and unclean, declared the ceremonial distinction of meats abolished in Christ; "every creature of God is good, and nothing to be refused" (1 Timothy 4:4); the food-law fulfilled and set aside; the believer free from the old distinctions. The witnesses observe the deeper meaning for the Gentiles: the command to eat the unclean figured the command to receive the Gentiles; as Peter was to count no food common, so he was to count no man common (Acts 10:28); the abolition of the food-law the sign of the abolition of the separation between Jew and Gentile. The expositors note the divine authority of the command: it was God's voice, commanding Peter; the new liberty not Peter's choice but God's command; the abolition of the old distinction declared by divine authority, that Peter might act on it without doubt. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the divine command to disregard the old distinction, the test of Peter's obedience, and the abolition of the separation it taught. The verse thus records the voice commanding Peter to slay and eat — to disregard the old distinction — teaching the divine abolition of the ceremonial separation and the admission of the Gentiles it figured.
8:
"But I said, Not so, Lord: for nothing common or unclean hath at any time entered into my mouth." The witnesses note Peter's reluctance and his scrupulous observance of the law. Gill observes that Peter "took the voice he heard to be the voice of the Lord, and yet he was not obedient to the heavenly vision, and even though he was an hungry; which shows what a strict observer he was of the ceremonies of the law, and how much he was prejudiced in favour of them." The witnesses note Peter's refusal — "Not so, Lord" — protesting that he had never eaten anything common or unclean; the depth of his attachment to the law and his prejudice against the unclean. The witnesses note Peter's reluctance. The witnesses note his scrupulous observance.

The witnesses note Peter's refusal — never having eaten the unclean — showing his deep attachment to the law.

So the witnesses make the verse Peter's reluctance. The lesson the expositors draw is the depth of Peter's attachment to the law and the prejudice that even a divine command had to overcome. The witnesses note the refusal: "Not so, Lord: for nothing common or unclean hath at any time entered into my mouth" — Peter, though commanded by the heavenly voice, refused; protesting that he had never in his life eaten anything common (ceremonially defiled) or unclean (forbidden by the law); his lifelong scruple holding him back even from obeying the voice. The expositors observe the striking inconsistency Gill marks: Peter took the voice to be the Lord's, yet was not obedient to it — even though he was hungry; a remarkable proof of how strict an observer he was of the ceremonies of the law, and how deeply prejudiced in their favour; the apostle's reverence for the law overcoming, for the moment, his obedience to the voice. The witnesses press the depth of the attachment this reveals: Peter's refusal shows how deeply the distinction of clean and unclean was ingrained — so deeply that even a direct command from the Lord met resistance; the prejudice of a lifetime, rooted in the law, not lightly overcome; the strength of inbred religious habit. The expositors draw the lesson on the difficulty of overcoming long-held prejudice: even an apostle, even at the Lord's command, found it hard to lay aside the distinction he had kept all his life; the prejudice so strong that the command had to be repeated three times (v.10); the lesson of how stubborn the old separation was, and how much divine teaching it took to overcome it. The witnesses observe the partial fault and the partial excuse in Peter: his refusal was a failure of obedience, a clinging to the old where God commanded the new; yet it sprang from reverence for the law, not from rebellion; the mixture of conscientiousness and prejudice in the godly. The expositors note the providence in recording this: Peter's reluctance, set down in his own account, shows the objectors that he too had shared their prejudice, and had been overcome only by the manifest will of God; his slowness an argument that the new liberty was God's command, not his invention; the apostle, once reluctant, now persuaded by God. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the depth of Peter's attachment to the law, the difficulty of overcoming long prejudice, and the divine teaching needed to set aside the old. The verse thus records Peter's refusal — Not so, Lord, I have never eaten the unclean — teaching the depth of his attachment to the law and the prejudice a divine command had to overcome.
9:
"But the voice answered me again from heaven, What God hath cleansed, that call not thou common." The witnesses note the heavenly reproof and the great principle. Gill notes the voice "answered him again" from heaven, the voice of the Lord, correcting his refusal. The witnesses note the great principle: "What God hath cleansed, that call not thou common" — God has cleansed what the law called unclean; Peter is not to count common or unclean what God has cleansed; the principle abolishing the old distinction and (deeply) declaring the Gentiles clean, received by God. The witnesses note the great principle. The witnesses note what God hath cleansed.

The witnesses note the great principle — what God hath cleansed, call not common — abolishing the old distinction.

So the witnesses make the verse the heavenly reproof and the great principle. The lesson the expositors draw is the abolition of the old distinction by God's cleansing, and the admission of the Gentiles it declared. The witnesses note the reproof: "the voice answered me again from heaven" — the heavenly voice, answering Peter's refusal, corrected him; God overruling the apostle's scruple, declaring the new truth against his prejudice. The expositors observe the great principle: "What God hath cleansed, that call not thou common" — God has cleansed what the law had called unclean; and Peter is not to count common or unclean what God has cleansed; the principle that abolishes the old distinction and establishes the new liberty. The witnesses press the meaning for the food-law: God has cleansed all meats, abolishing the distinction of clean and unclean; "unto the pure all things are pure" (Titus 1:15); the believer not to call common what God has cleansed; the ceremonial distinction of meats set aside. The expositors draw the deeper meaning for the Gentiles: the principle, applied (as Peter understood it, v.18; Acts 10:28), declares the Gentiles cleansed — no longer to be counted common or unclean, but received by God; what God has cleansed (the Gentiles, by the gospel) Peter is not to call common; the abolition of the separation between Jew and Gentile. The witnesses observe the ground of the cleansing: it is God who cleanses — by his own act, his own grace; the Gentiles cleansed not by the law, nor by becoming Jews, but by God's act in the gospel; the cleansing God's, the reception therefore not to be refused. The expositors note the authority of the principle: "what God hath cleansed" — the cleansing is God's, and therefore final; Peter, and the church, may not call common what God has declared clean; the divine act settling the matter, the human prejudice to yield to it. The witnesses draw the lesson on the abolition of the old distinctions: God, by his cleansing, has abolished the distinction of clean and unclean, both in food and (deeply) in peoples; the believer is to receive what God receives, to count clean what God has cleansed; the old separation done away in Christ. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the great principle — what God hath cleansed, call not common — the abolition of the old distinction, and the admission of the Gentiles it declared. The verse thus records the heavenly reproof and the great principle — what God hath cleansed — teaching the abolition of the old distinction by God's cleansing and the reception of the Gentiles whom God has cleansed.
10:
"And this was done three times: and all were drawn up again into heaven." The witnesses note the threefold repetition and the withdrawal of the sheet. Gill notes "the voice from heaven answered three times, and encouraged Peter to kill and eat." Ellicott and Robertson mark the vividness of Peter's word for the sheet being "drawn up" — a rapid upward movement, the detail of personal recollection. The witnesses note the vision repeated three times, then the sheet drawn back into heaven — the threefold repetition confirming the certainty and importance of the lesson, and overcoming Peter's reluctance. The witnesses note the threefold repetition. The witnesses note the withdrawal of the sheet.

The witnesses note the vision repeated three times — confirming the certainty of the lesson and overcoming Peter's reluctance.

So the witnesses make the verse the threefold repetition. The lesson the expositors draw is the certainty and importance of the lesson, confirmed by the threefold repetition, and the overcoming of Peter's reluctance. The witnesses note the repetition: "this was done three times" — the vision, with its command and the great principle, was repeated three times; Gill notes the voice answered three times, encouraging Peter to kill and eat; the threefold repetition impressing the lesson on the reluctant apostle. The expositors observe the purpose of the repetition: the lesson was so important, and so contrary to Peter's lifelong prejudice, that it was repeated three times — to confirm its certainty, to overcome his reluctance, to impress it indelibly; the threefold repetition the mark of a weighty and certain divine communication (as Pharaoh's dream was doubled, "because the thing is established by God," Genesis 41:32). The witnesses press the overcoming of Peter's reluctance: his refusal (v.8) was met by the threefold repetition; God patiently and emphatically pressing the lesson until the apostle's prejudice yielded; the persistence of the divine teaching overcoming the stubbornness of the old habit. The expositors draw the lesson on the certainty of the truth: the threefold repetition established the lesson beyond doubt — the old distinction abolished, the Gentiles to be received; the truth confirmed by the repetition, that Peter might act on it with full assurance; no room left for doubt or for clinging to the old. The witnesses note the withdrawal of the sheet: "all were drawn up again into heaven" — the sheet, with its creatures, was drawn back up into heaven; Ellicott and Robertson mark the vividness of Peter's word for the rapid upward movement; the vision ending, its lesson delivered. The expositors observe the completeness of the lesson: the vision, having taught its lesson three times, was withdrawn; the divine communication complete, the apostle instructed; the sheet returning to heaven whence it came, the lesson now to be acted on. The witnesses note the providence in the timing: as the vision ended, the men from Cornelius arrived (v.11); the vision and the messengers providentially joined, the lesson and its application brought together; God ordering the vision and the call to meet. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the certainty of the lesson confirmed by the threefold repetition, the overcoming of Peter's reluctance, and the providence joining the vision to its application. The verse thus records the threefold repetition and the withdrawal of the sheet — the vision confirmed three times — teaching the certainty and importance of the lesson and the overcoming of Peter's reluctance.
11:
"And, behold, immediately there were three men already come unto the house where I was, sent from Caesarea unto me." The witnesses note the providential arrival of Cornelius's messengers. Gill marks the timing: "the moment the vision was over" the three men were at the house; the vision and the messengers providentially joined. The witnesses note the three men from Cornelius arriving at the very moment the vision ended — the providence that joined the divine instruction to its immediate application, the vision interpreted by the arrival. The witnesses note the messengers' arrival. The witnesses note the providential timing.

The witnesses note the three messengers arriving as the vision ended — the providence joining instruction to application.

So the witnesses make the verse the providential arrival of the messengers. The lesson the expositors draw is the providence that joined the vision to its application, the messengers arriving at the very moment the lesson was given. The witnesses note the timing: "immediately there were three men already come unto the house" — Gill marks that the moment the vision ended, the three men sent from Cornelius were at the house; the vision and the messengers providentially joined, the divine instruction immediately followed by its application. The expositors observe the providence in this exact timing: God ordered the vision and the messengers to coincide — the vision teaching Peter that no man was to be called common, and the messengers arriving at once to call him to a Gentile; the lesson and its application brought together, that Peter might understand the vision by the call and obey the call by the vision. The witnesses press the interpretation the timing supplied: the vision, in itself somewhat obscure, was interpreted by the arrival of the Gentile messengers — Peter learning that the cleansing of the unclean meant the receiving of the Gentiles; the providence making the meaning plain by joining the sign to the event. The expositors draw the lesson on the providence guiding the church's mission: God ordered every detail — the vision, the messengers, the timing — that Peter might be led, without doubt, to the Gentiles; the divine guidance of the great step, leaving nothing to chance; the providence confirming the call. The witnesses observe the use of this in Peter's defense: Peter recounts the providential timing as evidence that his going to the Gentiles was of God — the vision and the messengers joined by God's hand, not by his own contrivance; the providence an argument that the mission was divine. The expositors note the encouragement to trust providence: God joins his instruction to its application, his leading to its occasion; the believer, taught by God, finds the providence that calls him to act on the teaching; the vision and the call meeting by God's ordering. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the providence that joined the vision to the messengers, the interpretation the timing supplied, and the divine guidance of the church's mission. The verse thus records the providential arrival of the messengers — three men come as the vision ended — teaching the providence that joined the divine instruction to its immediate application.
12:
"And the Spirit bade me go with them, nothing doubting. Moreover these six brethren accompanied me, and we entered into the man's house:" The witnesses note the Spirit's command and the six witnesses. Clarke notes the "six brethren" were "probably pointing to them, being present, as proper persons to confirm the truth of what he was delivering." Ellicott and Robertson note the Greek for "nothing doubting" (or "making no distinction") is the same word as "contended" (v.2) — Peter, guided by the Spirit, raised no debate such as the objectors were raising. The witnesses note the Spirit commanding Peter to go without doubting, and the six brethren who accompanied him as witnesses. The witnesses note the Spirit's command. The witnesses note the six witnesses.

The witnesses note the Spirit commanding Peter to go without doubting — with six brethren as witnesses to the truth.

So the witnesses make the verse the Spirit's command and the six witnesses. The lesson the expositors draw is the divine command that authorized Peter's going, and the witnesses who confirmed his account. The witnesses note the command: "the Spirit bade me go with them, nothing doubting" — the Holy Spirit directly commanded Peter to go with the messengers, without doubting or hesitation; the divine command authorizing his going to the Gentile, removing all ground for scruple. The expositors observe the force of "nothing doubting," which Ellicott and Robertson mark: the Greek is the same word as "contended" (v.2) — Peter, guided by the Spirit, raised no debate or dispute such as the objectors were now raising; the apostle, commanded by the Spirit, obeyed without the doubting and disputing that the circumcision party showed; a gentle reproof to their contention. The witnesses press the authority of the command: not Peter's choice, but the Spirit's command, sent him to the Gentiles; the going authorized by God, the apostle obeying the divine direction; the mission grounded in the command of the Spirit. The expositors draw the lesson on obedience without doubting: the Spirit commanded, and Peter obeyed without hesitation; the believer, when God's will is made plain, is to obey without doubting or disputing; the contrast between Peter's prompt obedience and the objectors' contention. The witnesses note the six witnesses: "these six brethren accompanied me" — six Jewish believers from Joppa went with Peter to Cornelius's house, and (Clarke notes) were probably present now, pointed to as witnesses to confirm the truth of Peter's account; the six the witnesses who could attest all that happened. The expositors observe the wisdom in the witnesses: Peter had taken six brethren, so that there should be credible witnesses to the great event — the conversion of the Gentiles, the falling of the Spirit; the witnesses confirming his account, removing all doubt of the facts; the truth established in the mouth of many witnesses (2 Corinthians 13:1). The witnesses draw the lesson on the confirmation of the truth: Peter's account rested not on his word alone, but on the testimony of seven (himself and the six); the facts attested, the objectors' doubt answered by the witnesses; the truth confirmed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Spirit's command authorizing the mission, the obedience without doubting, and the witnesses who confirmed the truth. The verse thus records the Spirit's command and the six witnesses — go nothing doubting, with the brethren — teaching the divine command that authorized Peter's going and the witnesses who confirmed his account.
13:
"And he shewed us how he had seen an angel in his house, which stood and said unto him, Send men to Joppa, and call for Simon, whose surname is Peter;" The witnesses note Cornelius's vision of the angel. Gill notes the clause "in his house" is fitly added, "for since an angel had entered into his house, it could not be criminal in Peter and the six brethren to follow him"; and discusses the angel being called both a man and an angel (appearing in the form of a man). The witnesses note Cornelius recounting the angel who directed him to send for Peter — the divine call on the Gentile's side, matching the vision on Peter's; God working at both ends to bring them together. The witnesses note Cornelius's angelic vision. The witnesses note the call for Peter.

The witnesses note Cornelius's angel directing him to send for Peter — God working at both ends to join them.

So the witnesses make the verse Cornelius's vision of the angel. The lesson the expositors draw is the divine call on the Gentile's side, matching the vision on Peter's, God working at both ends to bring the gospel to the Gentiles. The witnesses note the account: "he shewed us how he had seen an angel in his house, which stood and said unto him, Send men to Joppa, and call for Simon, whose surname is Peter" — Cornelius recounted to Peter and the brethren how an angel had appeared in his house, directing him to send for Peter; the divine call on the Gentile's side. The expositors observe the matching of the two visions: as Peter had his vision (the sheet), so Cornelius had his (the angel); God working at both ends — preparing Peter to go and Cornelius to send; the two divine communications meeting, the mission ordered by God on both sides; the providence joining the apostle and the seeker. The witnesses press Gill's note on "in his house": the clause is fitly added, for since an angel had entered Cornelius's house, it could not be criminal in Peter and the six brethren to follow him in; the angel's entrance sanctioning the apostles' entrance; the divine warrant for going in to the Gentile. The expositors draw the lesson on the divine origin of the Gentile call: Cornelius did not seek Peter of himself, but was directed by an angel; the call to send for Peter was God's, not Cornelius's contrivance; the whole proceeding, on the Gentile's side as on Peter's, the work of God; the Gentile mission divinely initiated at both ends. The witnesses note Gill's observation on the angel called both man and angel: the angel appeared in the form of a man (as angels often did), so Cornelius called him a man, while Luke and Peter call him an angel; the heavenly messenger in human form. The expositors observe the use of this in Peter's defense: Peter recounts Cornelius's angel as further evidence that the mission was of God — God directing the Gentile to send, the angel sanctioning the apostle's coming; the providence at both ends an argument that Peter's going was divinely ordered. The witnesses draw the lesson on God preparing both the messenger and the hearer: God prepares the preacher and the seeker, joining them by his providence; the gospel reaching the Gentile by God's working at both ends; the divine ordering of the means of salvation. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the divine call on the Gentile's side, the matching of the two visions, and God's working at both ends to bring the gospel to the Gentiles. The verse thus records Cornelius's vision of the angel — directing him to send for Peter — teaching the divine call on the Gentile's side and God's working at both ends to join the apostle and the seeker.
14:
"Who shall tell thee words, whereby thou and all thy house shall be saved." The witnesses note the promise of salvation by the preached word. Gill explains "words" as "doctrines, such as relate to the person and office of Christ, to peace and pardon, and righteousness and salvation by him; such as Peter did preach." Barnes notes "all thy house" — the family also received baptism and was saved; and that Cornelius "feared God with all his house" (Acts 10:2). The witnesses note the angel's promise that Peter's words — the gospel — would be the means of salvation for Cornelius and his whole household; salvation through the preached word, the household embraced. The witnesses note salvation by the word. The witnesses note the household saved.

The witnesses note the promise: Peter's words would save Cornelius and all his house — salvation by the preached gospel.

So the witnesses make the verse the promise of salvation by the preached word. The lesson the expositors draw is that salvation comes by the preached gospel, and that the promise embraced the whole household. The witnesses note the means of salvation named: "Who shall tell thee words, whereby thou and all thy house shall be saved" — the angel directed Cornelius to Peter, who would tell him "words" — the gospel; and by these words Cornelius and his house would be saved; salvation through the preaching of the word. The expositors observe what these "words" are, which Gill names: doctrines concerning the person and office of Christ, peace and pardon, righteousness and salvation by him — the gospel Peter preached; the words of life, the message of salvation. The witnesses press the great truth that salvation comes by the word: the angel, who might have preached the gospel himself, was not permitted to — but directed Cornelius to a man, Peter, who would speak the words of salvation; "faith cometh by hearing, and hearing by the word of God" (Romans 10:17); God saving by the preached word, through human instruments, not by angels. The expositors draw the lesson on the dignity of preaching: God has appointed the preaching of the word as the means of salvation; the angel directs Cornelius to a preacher, not preaching himself; "it pleased God by the foolishness of preaching to save them that believe" (1 Corinthians 1:21); the word the means, the preacher the instrument. The witnesses note the embrace of the household: "thou and all thy house shall be saved" — the promise extended to Cornelius's whole family; Barnes notes the family also received baptism and was saved, and that Cornelius feared God with all his house (Acts 10:2); the gospel embracing the household, the promise to the man and his family. The expositors observe the blessing of a godly household: Cornelius's whole house, fearing God and now saved by the word; the head of the house leading his family to the gospel; the promise of salvation to the believing household; the gospel blessing not the individual only but the family. The witnesses draw the lesson on salvation by the word and the household embraced: God saves by the preached gospel, through human preachers; and the promise reaches the whole house of the believer; the word the means, the family included. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks salvation by the preached word, the dignity of preaching, and the gospel's embrace of the household. The verse thus records the promise of salvation by the word — Peter's words saving Cornelius and his house — teaching that salvation comes by the preached gospel and embraces the whole household.
15:
"And as I began to speak, the Holy Ghost fell on them, as on us at the beginning." The witnesses note the falling of the Spirit on the Gentiles. Gill notes "begin" with Luke denotes the thing itself, not strictly the start — it was toward the close of Peter's discourse that this happened. Lightfoot discusses the hour. The witnesses note the Holy Ghost falling on the Gentile hearers as on the apostles at Pentecost — the decisive sign that God had received the Gentiles, given before baptism and without the laying on of hands; God's own seal on their reception. The witnesses note the Spirit falling on the Gentiles. The witnesses note the sign at Pentecost.

The witnesses note the Spirit falling on the Gentiles as at Pentecost — God's decisive seal on their reception.

So the witnesses make the verse the falling of the Spirit on the Gentiles. The lesson the expositors draw is the decisive sign of God's reception of the Gentiles, given as at Pentecost, the seal on their admission. The witnesses note the event: "as I began to speak, the Holy Ghost fell on them, as on us at the beginning" — while Peter was preaching, the Holy Ghost fell on the Gentile hearers, as he had fallen on the apostles at Pentecost ("at the beginning"); the same gift, the same sign, now given to the Gentiles. The expositors observe Gill's note on "began": with Luke, "begin" often denotes the thing itself, not strictly the start; it was toward the close of Peter's discourse, not its beginning, that the Spirit fell; the falling of the Spirit confirming and crowning the preaching. The witnesses press the decisiveness of the sign: the Holy Ghost fell on the Gentiles — the same Spirit, with the same visible gifts (speaking with tongues, Acts 10:46), as on the apostles at Pentecost; God's own act, his decisive seal on the reception of the Gentiles; the Spirit given before baptism and without the laying on of hands, that none might doubt it was God's doing. The expositors draw the lesson on God's seal of acceptance: the falling of the Spirit was God's manifest declaration that he had received the Gentiles — accepting them, granting them the same gift as the Jewish believers; the divine seal on their admission, beyond all dispute; God himself settling the question. The witnesses observe the parallel with Pentecost ("as on us at the beginning"): the Gentiles received the very same gift, in the same manner, as the apostles at the church's beginning; the Gentile Pentecost matching the Jewish; the equality of the Gentiles with the Jews in the gift of the Spirit; no difference made (v.12). The expositors note the use of this in Peter's defense: Peter's strongest argument was this — that God himself had given the Gentiles the Spirit, as he had given the apostles; the divine act answering all objection; "who was I, that I could withstand God?" (v.17). The witnesses draw the lesson on the divine settling of the question: the admission of the Gentiles was settled not by men, but by God, who gave them the Spirit; the church to receive whom God received; the Spirit's gift the proof of God's acceptance. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the falling of the Spirit on the Gentiles, God's decisive seal on their reception, and the divine settling of the question. The verse thus records the Spirit falling on the Gentiles — as on the apostles at Pentecost — teaching the decisive sign of God's reception of the Gentiles and the divine seal on their admission.
16:
"Then remembered I the word of the Lord, how that he said, John indeed baptized with water; but ye shall be baptized with the Holy Ghost." The witnesses note Peter's recollection of Christ's word about the baptism of the Spirit. Clarke draws a remarkable inference: Christ's words (Acts 1:5) "have been supposed by many to be referred to the apostles alone; but here it is evident that St. Peter believed they were a promise made to all Christians... whether Jews or Gentiles, who should believe on Jesus Christ"; so when the Spirit fell on the Gentiles, he saw it a fulfillment. The witnesses note Peter remembering Christ's promise of the baptism with the Holy Ghost, and recognizing its fulfillment in the Gentiles — the promise reaching to all believers. The witnesses note the word remembered. The witnesses note the baptism of the Spirit.

The witnesses note Peter recalling Christ's promise of the Spirit-baptism — fulfilled now in the Gentiles, the promise to all believers.

So the witnesses make the verse Peter's recollection of Christ's word. The lesson the expositors draw is that the promise of the baptism with the Spirit reaches to all believers, Jew and Gentile, fulfilled now in the Gentiles. The witnesses note the recollection: "Then remembered I the word of the Lord, how that he said, John indeed baptized with water; but ye shall be baptized with the Holy Ghost" — Peter, seeing the Spirit fall on the Gentiles, remembered Christ's promise (Acts 1:5) of the baptism with the Holy Ghost, surpassing John's baptism with water; the falling of the Spirit recalling and fulfilling the promise. The expositors observe Clarke's remarkable inference: Christ's words about the Spirit-baptism (Acts 1:5) had been supposed by many to refer to the apostles alone; but Peter here evidently believed they were a promise made to all Christians, Jew or Gentile, who should believe on Christ; for when the Spirit fell on the Gentiles, Peter saw it as a fulfillment of the promise — the promise thus reaching beyond the apostles to all believers. The witnesses press the breadth of the promise: the baptism with the Holy Ghost, promised by Christ, is for all believers — not the apostles only, nor the Jews only, but all who believe, Gentiles included; the Gentile reception of the Spirit the proof that the promise embraced them; the promise of the Spirit catholic, for all the church. The expositors draw the lesson on the surpassing of John's baptism: as at the first (Acts 1:5), so here, John's water-baptism is surpassed by the baptism with the Holy Ghost; the outward sign by the inward gift; the Gentiles receiving the greater baptism, the Spirit himself; the substance to which the water pointed. The witnesses observe the use of the remembered word in Peter's mind: the falling of the Spirit recalled Christ's promise, and the promise interpreted the event — Peter understanding the Gentile Pentecost as the fulfillment of Christ's word; the Scripture (Christ's word) and the event illuminating each other. The expositors note the comfort of the promise's breadth: the baptism with the Spirit is for all believers; the Gentile, the latest comer, receives the same promised gift; no believer excluded from the promise of the Spirit; the catholicity of the gift. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Christ's promise of the Spirit-baptism, its fulfillment in the Gentiles, and its reach to all believers. The verse thus records Peter's recollection of Christ's word — the baptism with the Holy Ghost — teaching the promise of the Spirit's baptism to all believers, fulfilled now in the Gentiles.
17:
"Forasmuch then as God gave them the like gift as he did unto us, who believed on the Lord Jesus Christ; what was I, that I could withstand God?" The witnesses note Peter's conclusive argument. Barnes presses the force: "what power or right had I to oppose the manifest will of God that the Gentiles should be received into the Christian church?... He had indicated his will; he had showed his intention to save the Gentiles; and the prejudices of Peter were all overcome." The witnesses note Peter's conclusion: since God gave the Gentiles the same gift as the believing Jews, who was Peter to withstand God? — the submission to God's manifest will, the decisive argument silencing all objection. The witnesses note Peter's conclusion. The witnesses note submission to God's will.

The witnesses note Peter's conclusive argument — who was he to withstand God, who gave the Gentiles the same gift?

So the witnesses make the verse Peter's conclusive argument. The lesson the expositors draw is the duty of submission to the manifest will of God, who had received the Gentiles by giving them the same gift. The witnesses note the argument: "Forasmuch then as God gave them the like gift as he did unto us, who believed on the Lord Jesus Christ; what was I, that I could withstand God?" — Peter reasons that since God gave the Gentiles the same gift of the Spirit as he gave the believing Jews, to oppose their reception would be to withstand God; the conclusive argument grounded in God's own act. The expositors press Barnes's statement of the force: what power or right had Peter to oppose the manifest will of God that the Gentiles should be received? God had indicated his will, shown his intention to save the Gentiles, by giving them the Spirit; and Peter's prejudices were overcome by the manifest act of God; to resist would be to fight against God. The witnesses press the ground of the argument: "God gave them the like gift" — the same Spirit, the same gift, as the believing Jews received; God making no difference; and since God had received them, by giving them his Spirit, the church must receive them; the divine act the warrant for the human reception. The expositors draw the lesson on submission to God's manifest will: when God has plainly shown his will — here, by giving the Gentiles the Spirit — the believer is to submit, not to resist; to withstand the manifest will of God is presumption and folly; "who hath resisted his will?" (Romans 9:19); the duty to yield to what God has plainly done. The witnesses observe the humility of Peter's "what was I?": the apostle counts himself nothing against the will of God — "what was I, that I could withstand God?"; the lowliness that submits to God's act, not presuming to oppose it; the servant yielding to the Master's manifest will. The expositors note the conclusiveness of the argument for the objectors: this was unanswerable — God had received the Gentiles; who could object? the divine act settling the matter beyond dispute; the objectors silenced by the manifest will of God (v.18). The witnesses draw the lesson on yielding to God's providence: when God's will is made plain by his acts, the believer is to yield, laying aside prejudice and objection; Peter's example of submission to the manifest will of God a pattern; the church to receive whom God receives. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Peter's conclusive argument, the duty of submission to God's manifest will, and the humility that withstands not God. The verse thus records Peter's conclusive argument — who was I to withstand God? — teaching the duty of submission to the manifest will of God, who had received the Gentiles by giving them his Spirit.
18:
"When they heard these things, they held their peace, and glorified God, saying, Then hath God also to the Gentiles granted repentance unto life." The witnesses note the objectors silenced and the great truth confessed. Barnes marks the result: "they held their peace... they were convinced, as Peter had been, by the manifest indications of the will of God"; and the great truth established — "that the doors of the church are opened to the entire Gentile world... it at once changed the views of the apostles... broke down all their long-cherished prejudices; taught them to look upon all men as their brethren." The witnesses note the circumcision party, convinced, ceasing their objection and glorifying God for granting the Gentiles repentance unto life. The witnesses note the objectors silenced. The witnesses note repentance unto life.

The witnesses note the objectors silenced and glorifying God — confessing that God had granted the Gentiles repentance unto life.

So the witnesses make the verse the objectors silenced and the great truth confessed. The lesson the expositors draw is the convincing power of God's manifest will, the door opened to the Gentiles, and repentance as God's gift. The witnesses note the result: "they held their peace, and glorified God" — the circumcision party, hearing Peter's account, ceased their objection and glorified God; convinced, as Peter had been, by the manifest indications of God's will; their prejudice yielding to the evidence of God's act. The expositors observe the change Barnes marks: the great truth was now established — that the doors of the church are opened to the entire Gentile world; the event changing the views of the apostles and early Christians, breaking down their long-cherished prejudices, teaching them to look on all men as brethren; the church's catholicity established by this manifest act of God. The witnesses press the confession: "Then hath God also to the Gentiles granted repentance unto life" — the objectors now confess that God has granted the Gentiles repentance unto life; the great truth of the Gentile inclusion, owned and glorified; the door of the church opened to all nations. The expositors draw the lesson on repentance as God's gift: "God hath... granted repentance unto life" — repentance is God's gift, granted by him; not the work of man alone, but the gift of God, who grants the Gentiles repentance leading to life; "God... to give repentance" (Acts 5:31; 2 Timothy 2:25); the grace of repentance bestowed by God. The witnesses observe the phrase "repentance unto life": the repentance God grants leads to life — eternal life; the saving repentance, not a barren remorse, but the turning that issues in life; the gift of God that brings the Gentiles to salvation. The expositors note the right response of the objectors: convinced, they did not resent or persist, but glorified God — yielding to the manifest will, rejoicing in the Gentile inclusion; the prejudice overcome, the truth embraced, God glorified; a pattern of yielding to God's manifest will. The witnesses draw the lesson on the door opened to the Gentiles: this verse marks the church's confession of the Gentile inclusion — the door opened, the Gentiles granted repentance unto life; the great turning-point owned and glorified; the gospel henceforth for all nations. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the objectors silenced and glorifying God, the door opened to the Gentiles, and repentance unto life as God's gift. The verse thus records the objectors silenced and the great truth confessed — God hath granted the Gentiles repentance unto life — teaching the convincing power of God's manifest will, the door opened to the Gentiles, and repentance as God's saving gift.
19:
"Now they which were scattered abroad upon the persecution that arose about Stephen travelled as far as Phenice, and Cyprus, and Antioch, preaching the word to none but unto the Jews only." The witnesses note the spread of the gospel by the scattered, at first only to Jews. Chrysostom marks the providence: "the persecution turned out to be no slight benefit, as to them that love God all things work together for good (Romans 8:28); if they had made it their express study how best to establish the Church, they would have done no other thing than this — they dispersed the teachers." The witnesses note the believers scattered by the persecution carrying the gospel as far as Phenice, Cyprus, and Antioch, but at first preaching only to the Jews — the gospel spreading, though not yet to the Gentiles. The witnesses note the scattered preaching. The witnesses note the gospel spread by persecution.

The witnesses note the scattered believers spreading the gospel to Phenice, Cyprus, and Antioch — at first only to Jews.

So the witnesses make the verse the spread of the gospel by the scattered believers. The lesson the expositors draw is the providence that spread the gospel through the persecution, carrying it far, though at first only to the Jews. The witnesses note the spread: "they which were scattered abroad upon the persecution that arose about Stephen travelled as far as Phenice, and Cyprus, and Antioch" — the believers scattered by the persecution (Acts 8:1) carried the gospel far, to Phenice, Cyprus, and Antioch; the dispersion spreading the word through the regions. The expositors press Chrysostom's marking of the providence: the persecution turned out to be no slight benefit, for to them that love God all things work together for good (Romans 8:28); had the believers made it their express study how best to establish the church, they could have done no better than this — the persecution dispersing the teachers, spreading the gospel far and wide; the enemies' assault the very means of the gospel's advance. The witnesses observe the great theme repeated: the persecution, meant to crush the church, scattered the believers, who carried the gospel wherever they went; God overruling the persecution to spread the gospel beyond Judea. The expositors note the limitation at first: "preaching the word to none but unto the Jews only" — the scattered believers, at first, preached only to the Jews (and Jewish proselytes); the gospel spreading among the Jews of the dispersion, but not yet (by these) to the Gentiles; the old limitation still holding, the great step to the Gentiles not yet taken by them. The witnesses press the significance of this limitation: the Gentile mission was a distinct act of God (through Peter and Cornelius, and then at Antioch, v.20), not the natural development of the scattered believers' work — the divine initiative in the Gentile mission. The expositors draw the lesson on the providence and the order of the gospel's advance: the gospel spread first among the Jews of the dispersion, then (v.20) to the Greeks at Antioch; the order — to the Jew first, then the Gentile (Romans 1:16); the providence ordering the gospel's advance step by step. The witnesses note the place of Antioch: the gospel reached Antioch, the great city that would become the center of the Gentile mission; the scattered believers preparing the ground; the providence bringing the gospel to the city of the first Gentile church. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the providence that spread the gospel through persecution, the gospel reaching far, and the order of its advance. The verse thus records the scattered believers spreading the gospel — to Phenice, Cyprus, and Antioch, at first only to Jews — teaching the providence that spread the gospel through persecution.
20:
"And some of them were men of Cyprus and Cyrene, which, when they were come to Antioch, spake unto the Grecians, preaching the Lord Jesus." The witnesses note the bold step of preaching to the Greeks at Antioch. Barnes notes the men of Cyprus and Cyrene, who spoke Greek, were led to "address the Grecians who were in Antioch"; preaching the Lord Jesus to them. Gill identifies some of these preachers. The witnesses note these scattered believers, at Antioch, preaching to the Greeks (Hellenists, whether Greek-speaking Jews or Gentiles) — a further step, the gospel reaching the Greek-speaking world; the founding of the great Antioch church. The witnesses note the preaching to the Greeks. The witnesses note the Antioch church begun.

The witnesses note the bold preaching to the Greeks at Antioch — a further step, the founding of the great Antioch church.

So the witnesses make the verse the bold step of preaching to the Greeks at Antioch. The lesson the expositors draw is the further advance of the gospel to the Greek-speaking world and the founding of the great Antioch church. The witnesses note the preachers: "some of them were men of Cyprus and Cyrene" — believers from Cyprus and Cyrene, Greek-speaking (Barnes notes), who, coming to Antioch, were led to address the Greeks there; their own background fitting them to reach the Greek-speaking population. The expositors observe the bold step: "spake unto the Grecians, preaching the Lord Jesus" — these believers, unlike the others who preached only to Jews (v.19), spoke to the Greeks (Hellenists — whether Greek-speaking Jews or, more likely here, Gentiles), preaching the Lord Jesus; a further step in the gospel's advance, reaching the Greek-speaking world. The witnesses note the significance of this step: at Antioch, the gospel was preached to the Greeks — a movement parallel to, and confirming, the reception of the Gentiles through Cornelius; the gospel breaking out to the Greek-speaking peoples; the founding of the church at Antioch, which would become the great center of the Gentile mission. The expositors draw the lesson on the advance of the gospel: from the Jews only (v.19) to the Greeks at Antioch; the gospel widening its reach, the Greek-speaking world embraced; the providence advancing the gospel step by step to all nations. The witnesses observe the substance of their preaching: "preaching the Lord Jesus" — they preached the Lord Jesus, his person, his lordship, the gospel of salvation in him; the message Christ-centered, the Lord Jesus the substance; the same gospel preached to the Greeks as to the Jews. The expositors note the founding of Antioch as a center: the gospel preached to the Greeks at Antioch founded the church that would become the base of the Gentile mission (the church that sent out Paul and Barnabas, Acts 13); the providence raising up at Antioch the center from which the gospel would go to the nations; the great Gentile church begun. The witnesses draw the lesson on the gospel reaching the Greek world: the bold step of preaching to the Greeks at Antioch widened the gospel's reach; the Greek-speaking world embraced; the church at Antioch the firstfruits and the center of the Gentile mission. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the bold preaching to the Greeks, the further advance of the gospel, and the founding of the great Antioch church. The verse thus records the bold preaching to the Greeks at Antioch — preaching the Lord Jesus — teaching the further advance of the gospel to the Greek-speaking world and the founding of the great Antioch church.
21:
"And the hand of the Lord was with them: and a great number believed, and turned unto the Lord." The witnesses note the power of God attending the preaching at Antioch. Gill expounds "the hand of the Lord" as not only his providence, love, and wisdom, but above all "his hand of power, the same with the arm of the Lord, which when revealed... the report of the Gospel is believed; but if that is not put forth, or efficacious grace is not exerted, no work is done... ministers labour in vain"; here the power of God attended their ministry with great success. The witnesses note God's power accompanying the preaching, so that a great number believed and turned to the Lord — efficacious grace at work. The witnesses note the hand of the Lord. The witnesses note the great conversion.

The witnesses note God's power attending the preaching — a great number believing and turning to the Lord.

So the witnesses make the verse the power of God attending the preaching at Antioch. The lesson the expositors draw is that the success of the gospel depends on the power of God, the hand of the Lord, which here wrought a great conversion. The witnesses note the cause of the success: "the hand of the Lord was with them" — Gill expounds this not only of God's providence, love, and wisdom guiding and protecting the preachers, but above all of his hand of power, the arm of the Lord, the efficacious grace that makes the gospel effectual; the power of God attending the preaching. The expositors press Gill's point on the necessity of this power: when the hand of the Lord, the arm of power, is revealed, the report of the gospel is believed; but if that power is not put forth, if efficacious grace is not exerted, no work is done, none are converted, ministers labour in vain and spend their strength for nought; the success of the gospel wholly dependent on the power of God. The witnesses press the result: "a great number believed, and turned unto the Lord" — the power of God attending the preaching, a great multitude believed and turned to the Lord; the conversion not of a few, but of many; the efficacious grace working a great harvest at Antioch. The expositors draw the lesson on the source of conversion: the conversion of sinners is the work of God's power, not of the preacher's eloquence or the hearer's will alone; the hand of the Lord makes the gospel effectual; "neither is he that planteth any thing, neither he that watereth; but God that giveth the increase" (1 Corinthians 3:7); the increase God's. The witnesses observe the two parts of conversion: "believed, and turned unto the Lord" — they believed (faith in Christ) and turned to the Lord (conversion, the turning of the heart and life); faith and turning together, the inward belief and the outward turning; the marks of true conversion. The expositors note the encouragement to the ministry: where the hand of the Lord is with the preachers, the gospel succeeds; the minister to depend on the power of God, not his own gifts; the great conversion at Antioch the fruit of God's power, encouraging the laborers to look to him. The witnesses draw the lesson on dependence: the success of the gospel is from the hand of the Lord; the preacher labours, but God gives the increase. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the power of God attending the preaching, the great conversion it wrought, and the dependence of all success on the hand of the Lord. The verse thus records the power of God attending the preaching — the hand of the Lord, a great number believing — teaching that the success of the gospel depends on the power of God, which here wrought a great conversion.
22:
"Then tidings of these things came unto the ears of the church which was in Jerusalem: and they sent forth Barnabas, that he should go as far as Antioch." The witnesses note the sending of Barnabas from Jerusalem. Barnes notes the church at Jerusalem heard of the remarkable conversion of the Gentiles and the success at Antioch, and "sent forth" Barnabas to aid the disciples and give their sanction — as they had done in the revival in Samaria (Acts 8:14); and that Barnabas, a native of Cyprus, was "peculiarly qualified for the work." The witnesses note the Jerusalem church, hearing of the great work at Antioch, sending Barnabas to confirm and aid it — the apostolic care extending to the new church. The witnesses note the sending of Barnabas. The witnesses note the church's care.

The witnesses note the Jerusalem church sending Barnabas to Antioch — extending its care to confirm the new work.

So the witnesses make the verse the sending of Barnabas. The lesson the expositors draw is the care of the Jerusalem church for the new work and the fitness of Barnabas for the task. The witnesses note the occasion: "tidings of these things came unto the ears of the church which was in Jerusalem" — the report of the great conversion at Antioch reached the mother church; the news of the gospel's success among the Greeks naturally exciting interest at Jerusalem. The expositors observe the sending: "they sent forth Barnabas, that he should go as far as Antioch" — the Jerusalem church sent Barnabas to Antioch, to aid the disciples and give the church's sanction; Barnes notes this was like their sending Peter and John to the revival in Samaria (Acts 8:14); the apostolic care extending to the new work, confirming and embracing it. The witnesses press the care of the mother church: the Jerusalem church did not neglect the new work, but sent a trusted man to confirm, aid, and oversee it; the care of the established church for the new converts; the unity of the church maintained, the new work joined to the whole. The expositors draw the lesson on the fitness of Barnabas, which Barnes marks: Barnabas, a native of Cyprus (Acts 4:36), was peculiarly qualified for the work — acquainted with the region, of generous and gracious character (v.24); the right man sent for the task; the wisdom of the church in its choice. The witnesses observe the providence in sending Barnabas: a good man, full of the Holy Ghost and faith (v.24), was sent — one who would rejoice in the grace of God, encourage the converts, and (v.25) bring Saul to the work; the providence supplying the new church with the right leader; the man fitted to the work. The expositors note the purpose of the sending: to confirm the work, to give the church's sanction, to aid and oversee the new converts; the establishing of the Antioch church under the care of the mother church; the new work confirmed and joined to the whole. The witnesses draw the lesson on the care of the church for new converts: the established church is to care for, confirm, and aid the new work; the unity and oversight of the whole church; the new converts not left alone, but embraced and helped. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the care of the Jerusalem church, the fitness of Barnabas, and the confirming of the new work. The verse thus records the sending of Barnabas — to confirm the work at Antioch — teaching the care of the church for the new work and the fitness of Barnabas for the task.
23:
"Who, when he came, and had seen the grace of God, was glad, and exhorted them all, that with purpose of heart they would cleave unto the Lord." The witnesses note Barnabas's joy at the grace of God and his exhortation. Gill explains "the grace of God" he saw as "the many instances of the powerful and efficacious grace of God in regeneration and conversion... the great goodness, love, and favour of God in enlightening, quickening, and converting so many souls"; the grace visible in its effects. The witnesses note Barnabas, seeing the evident grace of God in the converts, rejoicing, and exhorting them all to cleave to the Lord with purpose of heart — the encouragement of the gracious man. The witnesses note Barnabas's joy. The witnesses note the exhortation to cleave.

The witnesses note Barnabas glad at the grace of God — exhorting the converts to cleave to the Lord with purpose of heart.

So the witnesses make the verse Barnabas's joy and exhortation. The lesson the expositors draw is the joy of the gracious man at the grace of God, and the exhortation to steadfast cleaving to the Lord. The witnesses note what Barnabas saw: "had seen the grace of God" — Gill explains this as the evident effects of God's grace in the converts: the many instances of efficacious grace in regeneration and conversion, the goodness and favour of God in enlightening, quickening, and converting so many souls; the grace of God visible in its fruits. The expositors observe Barnabas's joy: "was glad" — Barnabas, seeing the grace of God in the converts, rejoiced; the gracious man glad at the work of God, rejoicing in the conversion of souls, not envying or doubting the new work, but glad; the joy of a heart that loves the grace of God. The witnesses press the character of Barnabas in his joy: a good man (v.24) rejoices at the grace of God in others; the absence of envy or party spirit; Barnabas glad that the Gentiles were converted, that the grace of God was at work; the generous, gracious heart.
