The expositors draw the lesson on the exhortation: "exhorted them all, that with purpose of heart they would cleave unto the Lord" — Barnabas exhorted the converts to cleave to the Lord, to hold fast to Christ, with purpose of heart — with settled resolve, firm determination, steadfast commitment; the exhortation to perseverance and steadfastness. The witnesses observe the meaning of "with purpose of heart": not a wavering or half-hearted attachment, but a settled, determined cleaving; the heart fixed, resolved to hold fast to the Lord; the steadfastness of true commitment; "cleave unto the Lord" (cf. Joshua 23:8). The expositors note the wisdom of the exhortation: new converts need to be exhorted to perseverance, to cleave to the Lord; the danger of falling away, of wavering; Barnabas, the encourager (his name means "son of consolation," Acts 4:36), exhorting them to steadfastness; the pastoral care of establishing the new converts. The witnesses draw the lesson on joy in the grace of God and steadfast cleaving: the believer is to rejoice at the grace of God in others, and to cleave to the Lord with purpose of heart; the joy of Barnabas and his exhortation a pattern. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Barnabas's joy at the grace of God, his exhortation to cleave to the Lord, and the steadfastness of true commitment. The verse thus records Barnabas's joy and exhortation — glad at the grace of God, exhorting them to cleave to the Lord — teaching the joy of the gracious man and the exhortation to steadfast cleaving.
24:
"For he was a good man, and full of the Holy Ghost and of faith: and much people was added unto the Lord." The witnesses note the character of Barnabas and the fruit of his ministry. Gill describes him: "he had the grace of God wrought in his soul, and did good works; he was very kind, and generous, and charitable," having sold his land for the church (Acts 4:37). The witnesses note Barnabas a good man, full of the Holy Ghost and of faith — the threefold commendation of his character; and the fruit, much people added to the Lord through his ministry. The witnesses note the character of Barnabas. The witnesses note much people added.

The witnesses note Barnabas a good man, full of the Holy Ghost and faith — and much people added to the Lord through him.

So the witnesses make the verse the character of Barnabas and the fruit of his ministry. The lesson the expositors draw is the threefold commendation of Barnabas's character and the fruit of a gracious ministry. The witnesses note the commendation: "he was a good man, and full of the Holy Ghost and of faith" — a threefold description of Barnabas's character: a good man (gracious, kind, generous), full of the Holy Ghost (filled with the Spirit, his gifts and graces), and full of faith (strong in believing, trusting God); the marks of an excellent servant of Christ. The expositors observe Gill's expansion of "a good man": Barnabas had the grace of God in his soul and did good works; he was kind, generous, and charitable, having sold his land and given the price to the church (Acts 4:37); the goodness of a gracious, liberal heart. The witnesses press the threefold character as the ground of his usefulness: "for he was a good man" — the "for" connecting his character to the fruit of his ministry; because he was good, full of the Spirit and faith, much people were added; the character of the minister bearing on the fruit of his work; the good and Spirit-filled man fruitful in the Lord's service. The expositors draw the lesson on the qualifications of a fruitful minister: goodness (a gracious, holy character), fullness of the Holy Ghost (the Spirit's power and gifts), and faith (strong trust in God); these the marks of one God uses; the character and the fruit joined. The witnesses note the fruit: "much people was added unto the Lord" — through Barnabas's ministry, much people were added to the Lord and the church; the conversion of many; the fruit of a gracious, Spirit-filled, faithful ministry; the harvest of his labour. The expositors observe the connection of character and fruit: the good, Spirit-filled, believing man was fruitful; God blessing the ministry of such a man with the addition of many; the character of the servant and the blessing of God joined in the fruit. The witnesses draw the lesson on the importance of character in ministry: the minister's character — goodness, the Spirit's fullness, faith — bears on his usefulness; God uses the good and Spirit-filled; the believer to seek such a character, that his service may be fruitful. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the threefold character of Barnabas, the fruit of his gracious ministry, and the importance of character in the Lord's service. The verse thus records the character of Barnabas and the fruit of his ministry — a good man, full of the Holy Ghost and faith, much people added — teaching the threefold commendation of his character and the fruit of a gracious ministry.
25:
"Then departed Barnabas to Tarsus, for to seek Saul:" The witnesses note Barnabas seeking Saul for the work at Antioch. Barnes infers the reason: the great success at Antioch, the revival, called for additional labour — "in such scenes the ministers of the gospel need additional help, as men in harvest-time seek the aid of others"; Saul was in the vicinity (Acts 9:30), eminently fitted to aid, and known to Barnabas (Acts 9:27). The witnesses note Barnabas going to Tarsus to seek Saul — the generous man bringing in the great apostle to share the work; the providence joining Barnabas and Saul. The witnesses note Barnabas seeking Saul. The witnesses note the need of additional labour.

The witnesses note Barnabas seeking Saul at Tarsus — bringing in the great apostle to share the abundant work.

So the witnesses make the verse Barnabas seeking Saul. The lesson the expositors draw is the need of additional labour in a great work and the generous wisdom of Barnabas in bringing in Saul. The witnesses note the action: "Barnabas departed to Tarsus, for to seek Saul" — Barnabas, finding the work at Antioch great and growing, went to Tarsus (where Saul had withdrawn, Acts 9:30) to seek him out and bring him to share the work. The expositors observe Barnes's inference about the reason: the great success and revival at Antioch called for additional labour — as in harvest-time the reapers seek the aid of others; the work too great for Barnabas alone, he sought help; Saul, in the vicinity, eminently fitted, and known to Barnabas (who had introduced him to the apostles, Acts 9:27), was the man. The witnesses press the generosity and wisdom of Barnabas: rather than keeping the great and fruitful work to himself, Barnabas sought out Saul — a greater man than himself — to share it; the absence of jealousy or self-seeking; the generous heart that seeks the best help for the work, not its own preeminence; the wisdom that brings in the gifted to the harvest. The expositors draw the lesson on the need of fellow-labourers: a great work needs many hands; the minister is to seek help when the harvest is great, not to labour alone; the wisdom of Barnabas in seeking Saul a pattern of cooperation in the Lord's work. The witnesses observe the providence in joining Barnabas and Saul: God brought together these two, who would become the great missionary pair (Acts 13); the providence using Barnabas to bring Saul into the work, the partnership that would carry the gospel to the Gentiles; the joining of the two for the great mission. The expositors note the magnanimity of Barnabas in particular: he sought Saul, who would soon eclipse him in greatness and labour; the generous man content to be the means of bringing in a greater; the self-effacing love that seeks the work's good above its own honour; "in honour preferring one another" (Romans 12:10). The witnesses draw the lesson on cooperation and generosity in ministry: the need of fellow-labourers, the wisdom of seeking the gifted, the generosity that seeks the work's good above self; Barnabas's example a pattern. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the need of additional labour, the generous wisdom of Barnabas, and the providence joining him with Saul. The verse thus records Barnabas seeking Saul — to share the work at Antioch — teaching the need of fellow-labourers and the generous wisdom of Barnabas in bringing in the great apostle.
26:
"And when he had found him, he brought him unto Antioch. And it came to pass, that a whole year they assembled themselves with the church, and taught much people. And the disciples were called Christians first in Antioch." The witnesses note the year of teaching and the origin of the name "Christians." Barnes notes Antioch was an important city, and the apostles directed their attention chiefly to cities as centres of influence; the year of teaching establishing the church. On the name, the witnesses note the disciples were first called "Christians" at Antioch — whether by their enemies in derision, by themselves, or providentially given; the name marking their distinctness, derived from Christ. The witnesses note the year of teaching. The witnesses note the name Christians.

The witnesses note the year of teaching at Antioch and the disciples first called Christians — the name derived from Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the year of teaching and the origin of the name "Christians." The lesson the expositors draw is the establishing of the Antioch church and the significance of the name derived from Christ. The witnesses note the year of teaching: "a whole year they assembled themselves with the church, and taught much people" — Barnabas and Saul spent a whole year at Antioch, assembling with the church and teaching the people; the diligent, settled instruction establishing the new church. The expositors observe Barnes's note on the importance of Antioch: a great city, and the apostles directed their attention chiefly to cities as centres of influence and power (as Paul stayed years at Ephesus and Corinth); the year at Antioch establishing the church in the great city, the future base of the Gentile mission. The witnesses press the great fact: "the disciples were called Christians first in Antioch" — the name "Christians" was first given to the disciples at Antioch; the believers, who had been called disciples, brethren, saints, now named "Christians" — from Christ. The expositors weigh the origin of the name. Some hold it was given by the enemies or the heathen of Antioch in derision or contempt — a nickname for the followers of Christ; others, that the disciples took it themselves, or that it was providentially given, a fit name marking them as Christ's. The witnesses note the significance whatever its origin: the name "Christians" derives from Christ — marking the disciples as his followers, belonging to him, named after him; the name a confession that they were of Christ, his people; "if any man suffer as a Christian, let him not be ashamed" (1 Peter 4:16). The expositors draw the lesson on the name: whether given in scorn or in honour, the name "Christians" became the honoured title of Christ's people — those named after him, bearing his name; the believer a Christian, of Christ, called by his name; the name to be worn worthily. The witnesses observe the fitness of the name arising at Antioch: where the gospel had broken out to the Gentiles, where Jew and Greek were united in one church, a new name was fitting — not "Jews," not a sect, but "Christians," from Christ; the name marking the new, catholic people of God. The witnesses draw the lesson on bearing the name of Christ: the believer is a Christian, named after Christ; the name a privilege and a calling, to be worn worthily, in confession of belonging to him. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the year of teaching at Antioch, the origin of the name "Christians," and the privilege of bearing the name of Christ. The verse thus records the year of teaching and the name "Christians" — first given at Antioch — teaching the establishing of the church and the honoured name derived from Christ.
27:
"And in these days came prophets from Jerusalem unto Antioch." The witnesses note the coming of prophets to Antioch. Gill notes that some time in that year, Christian prophets came from Jerusalem to Antioch; "for such there were in the Christian church, who had not only a gift of expounding the more mysterious prophecies of the Old Testament, but also of foretelling things to come." The witnesses note prophets — men endowed with the gift of prophecy, including foretelling future events — coming from Jerusalem to Antioch; the gift of prophecy in the apostolic church, the link between the mother church and the new. The witnesses note the prophets. The witnesses note the gift of prophecy.

The witnesses note prophets coming from Jerusalem to Antioch — the gift of prophecy in the apostolic church.

So the witnesses make the verse the coming of prophets to Antioch. The lesson the expositors draw is the gift of prophecy in the apostolic church and the link between the mother church and the new. The witnesses note the coming: "in these days came prophets from Jerusalem unto Antioch" — during the year of teaching, Christian prophets came from Jerusalem to Antioch; men endowed with the gift of prophecy, visiting the new church. The expositors observe Gill's description of these prophets: such there were in the Christian church — men who had not only the gift of expounding the more mysterious prophecies of the Old Testament, but also of foretelling things to come; the gift of prophecy including both interpretation and prediction; the prophets of the apostolic age. The witnesses press the reality of the prophetic gift in the early church: prophecy was one of the gifts of the Spirit (1 Corinthians 12:10; 14:1); men were given the gift of prophesying — speaking by the Spirit, expounding the Scriptures, and (as Agabus here, v.28) foretelling future events; the extraordinary gift of the apostolic age. The expositors draw the lesson on the prophets' role: the prophets edified the church, expounding the Scriptures and revealing things to come; their gift serving the church's instruction and (here) its preparation for coming events; the prophetic gift a help to the early church. The witnesses observe the link between Jerusalem and Antioch: the prophets came from Jerusalem to Antioch — the mother church and the new church connected, the fellowship and communication between them; the prophets a bond of unity, carrying gifts and tidings between the churches; the one church, though in many places. The expositors note the providence in their coming: the prophets came at the time when the famine was approaching, that Agabus might foretell it and the church prepare (v.28-29); the providence sending the prophets to warn and prepare; the prophetic gift serving the church's preparation and charity. The witnesses draw the lesson on the gifts of the Spirit in the early church: prophecy among them, given for the church's edification and guidance; the apostolic church endowed with extraordinary gifts; the prophets serving the church. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the gift of prophecy in the apostolic church, the prophets coming to Antioch, and the link between the churches. The verse thus records the coming of prophets to Antioch — from Jerusalem — teaching the gift of prophecy in the apostolic church and the link between the mother church and the new.
28:
"And there stood up one of them named Agabus, and signified by the Spirit that there should be great dearth throughout all the world: which came to pass in the days of Claudius Caesar." The witnesses note Agabus's prophecy of the famine and its fulfilment. Gill notes Agabus was "an itinerant prophet, who went from place to place delivering out his prophecies"; he signified by the Spirit a great dearth, fulfilled under Claudius. The witnesses note Agabus, by the Spirit, foretelling a great famine throughout the (Roman) world, which came to pass in the reign of Claudius — the prophecy verified by the event, the Spirit's foreknowledge confirmed. The witnesses note Agabus's prophecy. The witnesses note the famine foretold and fulfilled.

The witnesses note Agabus foretelling the famine by the Spirit — fulfilled under Claudius, the prophecy verified by the event.

So the witnesses make the verse Agabus's prophecy of the famine. The lesson the expositors draw is the Spirit's foreknowledge in the prophecy and its verification by the event, and the providence in the warning. The witnesses note the prophet and his prophecy: "one of them named Agabus... signified by the Spirit that there should be great dearth throughout all the world" — Agabus (Gill notes, an itinerant prophet), standing up, foretold by the Spirit a great famine throughout all the (Roman) world; the prophecy given by the Spirit, foretelling a coming dearth. The expositors observe the fulfilment: "which came to pass in the days of Claudius Caesar" — the famine came to pass in the reign of Claudius; the prophecy verified by the event (historically attested famines occurred under Claudius); the Spirit's foreknowledge confirmed, the prediction made good. The witnesses press the proof of the prophetic gift: the prophecy was by the Spirit, and the event verified it; the foretelling of a future famine, beyond human knowledge, fulfilled in the event; the reality of the Spirit's foreknowledge and the genuineness of the prophetic gift attested by the fulfilment. The expositors draw the lesson on the Spirit's foreknowledge: God, by his Spirit, foreknows and foretells the future; the prophecy of the famine a token of the Spirit's omniscience; the gift of prophecy resting on the foreknowledge of God; the prediction and its fulfilment confirming the divine origin of the gift. The witnesses observe the providence in the warning: the famine was foretold beforehand, that the church might prepare and provide relief (the next verses); the prophecy serving the church's charity, warning of the coming need; the providence using the prophetic gift to prepare the church for the famine; the foreknowledge serving practical love. The expositors note the scope "throughout all the world": the Roman world (the oikoumenē), the empire; a widespread dearth, affecting many regions, including Judea; the famine extensive, the need great. The witnesses draw the lesson on prophecy verified and providence in warning: the Spirit foretells, the event fulfils, the prophecy proved true; and God warns beforehand, that his people may prepare; the gift serving the church's foresight and charity. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Agabus's prophecy by the Spirit, its fulfilment under Claudius, and the providence in the warning. The verse thus records Agabus's prophecy of the famine — by the Spirit, fulfilled under Claudius — teaching the Spirit's foreknowledge verified by the event and the providence in the warning.
29:
"Then the disciples, every man according to his ability, determined to send relief unto the brethren which dwelt in Judaea:" The witnesses note the charity of the Antioch disciples. Barnes notes "according to his ability" means "according as they had prospered" — not that they were rich, but each gave as he could afford; and that they felt particularly bound to aid their Jewish brethren, from whom they had received spiritual things (Romans 15:25-27; 1 Corinthians 16:1-2). The witnesses note the disciples at Antioch, each according to his means, resolving to send relief to the believers in Judea — the charity and unity of the church, the Gentile converts aiding the Jewish brethren. The witnesses note the determined relief. The witnesses note the church's charity.

The witnesses note the Antioch disciples resolving to relieve the Judean brethren — each according to his ability.

So the witnesses make the verse the charity of the Antioch disciples. The lesson the expositors draw is the practical charity and unity of the church, each giving according to his ability to relieve the brethren. The witnesses note the resolve: "the disciples... determined to send relief unto the brethren which dwelt in Judaea" — the Antioch disciples, hearing of the coming famine, resolved to send relief to the believers in Judea; the practical charity moved by the prophecy, preparing to meet the need. The expositors observe the measure of the giving, which Barnes marks: "every man according to his ability" — each gave according as he had prospered, as he could afford; not that they were rich, but each contributed according to his means; the proportionate giving, each as he was able; the principle of charity according to ability (cf. 1 Corinthians 16:2, "as God hath prospered him"). The witnesses press the spirit of the charity: the disciples did not wait until the famine struck, but, warned by the prophecy, prepared beforehand; the foresight of love, providing against the coming need; the practical wisdom and generosity of the church. The expositors draw the lesson on the unity and mutual care of the church: the Antioch believers (largely Gentile converts) resolved to aid the Judean brethren (Jewish believers); the new church helping the mother church; the unity of the body, the members caring for one another; the bond of charity across the old division of Jew and Gentile. The witnesses observe the obligation Barnes notes: the Gentile converts felt particularly bound to aid their Jewish brethren, from whom they had received spiritual things; the New Testament enforcing this obligation — that those who received the gospel from the Jews should minister to their temporal needs (Romans 15:25-27; "their debtors they are"); the spiritual debt repaid in temporal aid. The expositors note the beauty of the charity: the Gentile church, lately come to the faith, aiding the Jewish church in need; the gospel uniting Jew and Gentile in mutual love; the practical fruit of the gospel in charity across the old barrier. The witnesses draw the lesson on practical charity: the church is to relieve the needs of the brethren, each according to his ability; the foresight and generosity of the Antioch disciples a pattern; the unity of the body shown in mutual aid. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the charity of the Antioch disciples, giving according to ability, and the unity of the church in mutual care. The verse thus records the charity of the Antioch disciples — resolving to relieve the Judean brethren — teaching the practical charity and unity of the church, each giving according to his ability.
30:
"Which also they did, and sent it to the elders by the hands of Barnabas and Saul." The witnesses note the relief carried out and the first mention of elders. Gill notes they not only determined but acted, putting their resolution into execution. Barnes marks this as "the first mention which we have in the New Testament of elders, or presbyters, in the Christian church" — the relief sent to the elders (presbyters) at Jerusalem, the officers who would distribute it; carried by Barnabas and Saul. The witnesses note the Antioch church performing its charity, sending the relief by Barnabas and Saul to the elders in Judea — the resolution carried out, the church order shown in the elders. The witnesses note the relief carried out. The witnesses note the elders.

The witnesses note the relief carried out — sent to the elders by Barnabas and Saul, the first mention of church elders.

So the witnesses make the verse the relief carried out and the first mention of elders. The lesson the expositors draw is the church's performance of its charity and the order of the church shown in the elders. The witnesses note the performance: "which also they did" — Gill marks that the disciples not only determined but acted, putting their resolution into execution; the charity not merely resolved but performed; the resolve carried out in deed. The expositors press the lesson on performing what is resolved: the Antioch disciples did what they determined; the charity completed, not left in good intentions; "to him that knoweth to do good, and doeth it not, to him it is sin" (James 4:17); the resolution made good in action; a pattern of charity carried out. The witnesses note the means of sending: "sent it to the elders by the hands of Barnabas and Saul" — the relief was sent to the elders at Jerusalem, carried by Barnabas and Saul; the two trusted men entrusted with the charity, conveying it to the proper hands for distribution. The expositors observe Barnes's note on the elders: this is the first mention in the New Testament of "elders" (presbyters) in the Christian church; the relief sent to the elders, the officers who would receive and distribute it; the order of the church shown in the elders, who managed its affairs and charity. The witnesses press the significance of the elders: the church had officers — elders (presbyters) — who oversaw and managed its affairs, including the distribution of relief; the order and government of the church appearing here; the elders the appointed officers for such administration. The expositors draw the lesson on church order: the church was not a disorderly body, but had officers — elders — for its government and the management of its charity; the order and government of the church appointed for its good; the elders entrusted with the relief, the proper channel for its distribution. The witnesses observe the trust reposed in Barnabas and Saul: the charity entrusted to two faithful men, who carried it to Jerusalem; the proper, careful conveyance of the relief by trusted hands; the integrity and order in handling the church's charity. The witnesses draw the lesson on charity performed and church order: the church performs its charity, by trusted hands, to the proper officers; the resolution carried out, the order maintained; the elders the appointed channel. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the charity performed, the relief sent by trusted hands to the elders, and the order of the church. The verse thus records the relief carried out — sent to the elders by Barnabas and Saul — teaching the church's performance of its charity and the order of the church shown in the elders.
